era_true.statement: Rome was founded as a kingdom before becoming a republic.
The Roman Republic preceded the Roman Empire.
Julius Caesar was assassinated in the Senate on the Ides of March.
Augustus was the first Roman emperor.
The Roman Empire was divided into eastern and western halves.
Constantinople was founded by Emperor Constantine.
The city of Constantinople sits on the Bosporus strait.
The Western Roman Empire fell in the year 476.
The Eastern Roman Empire outlasted the Western by nearly a thousand years.
Constantinople fell to the Ottoman Turks in 1453.
Sultan Mehmed II conquered Constantinople.
The Ottoman Empire controls the eastern Mediterranean sea routes.
Venice is a republic governed by a doge and a council of nobles.
Florence is ruled by the Medici family through influence over its institutions.
Lorenzo de Medici was known as a great patron of artists and scholars.
The Papal States occupy a large territory in central Italy.
The Pope holds both spiritual authority and temporal political power.
Milan is a major duchy in northern Italy.
Naples is a kingdom in southern Italy contested by foreign powers.
France and Spain have both claimed the throne of Naples.
King Charles VIII of France invaded Italy in 1494.
The Italian peninsula is divided among multiple competing states.
Alliances among Italian states shift frequently based on the balance of power.
Ambassadors reside permanently in foreign courts to gather intelligence and negotiate.
Venice maintains a sophisticated system of permanent ambassadors abroad.
Mercenary captains called condottieri lead armies for hire in Italy.
A prince who relies entirely on mercenaries risks betrayal.
Fortified castles and walled cities are the primary means of territorial defense.
Gunpowder weapons have changed the nature of siege warfare.
Cannon fire can breach walls that once withstood months of siege.
The crossbow and the longbow are both effective battlefield weapons.
Infantry armed with pikes can defeat mounted cavalry charges.
Naval galleys powered by oars dominate Mediterranean warfare.
Control of sea trade routes is essential to Venetian wealth.
The Silk Road connects Europe to China through Central Asia.
Spices from the East are among the most valuable trade goods in Europe.
Portugal has established sea routes along the western coast of Africa.
Portuguese sailors have rounded the Cape of Good Hope at Africa's southern tip.
Genoa and Venice have long competed for dominance in Mediterranean trade.
Gold and salt are major trade goods crossing the Sahara Desert.
The Hanseatic League controls much of the trade in the Baltic Sea.
Wool is a major English export commodity.
Florence's wealth derives significantly from banking and the wool trade.
Letters of credit allow merchants to conduct business without transporting gold.
The Medici bank has branches in multiple European cities.
Taxation and control of trade revenues sustain the power of princes.
A ruler who impoverishes his subjects weakens his own state.
Cicero wrote extensively on rhetoric, duty, and the Roman Republic.
Aristotle's works address politics, ethics, natural philosophy, and logic.
Plato founded the Academy in Athens.
Virgil wrote the Aeneid, an epic poem about the founding of Rome.
Livy wrote a comprehensive history of Rome from its founding.
Thucydides wrote a history of the war between Athens and Sparta.
The Peloponnesian War was fought between Athens and Sparta.
Alexander the Great conquered the Persian Empire.
Alexander the Great was tutored by Aristotle.
The library of Alexandria was one of the greatest centers of learning in the ancient world.
Ptolemy wrote the Almagest, a major work on astronomy.
Ptolemy's geography maps the known world using a coordinate system.
Euclid wrote the Elements, a foundational text on geometry.
Galen's medical writings have guided physicians for over a thousand years.
Hippocrates is regarded as the father of medicine.
The four humors are blood, phlegm, yellow bile, and black bile.
The Earth is a sphere, as demonstrated by Greek philosophers and astronomers.
The sun, moon, and five visible planets move across the sky.
Eclipses of the sun occur when the moon passes between the sun and the Earth.
The Nile River floods annually and makes the surrounding land fertile.
Egypt was ruled by pharaohs for thousands of years before the Roman conquest.
The pyramids of Egypt are ancient stone monuments of enormous size.
Jerusalem is a holy city to Christians, Muslims, and Jews.
The Crusades were military campaigns to reclaim the Holy Land from Muslim control.
The First Crusade captured Jerusalem in 1099.
Saladin recaptured Jerusalem from the Crusaders in 1187.
The Black Death killed a large portion of Europe's population in the fourteenth century.
The Black Death reached Italy by 1348.
The Great Schism divided the Western Church between rival popes.
The Council of Constance ended the Great Schism and elected Pope Martin V.
The Holy Roman Empire encompasses many German-speaking territories and principalities.
The Holy Roman Emperor is elected by a group of prince-electors.
The King of France rules from Paris and claims authority over French-speaking lands.
England and France fought a prolonged conflict known as the Hundred Years' War.
Joan of Arc led French forces to victory at Orléans.
The printing press using movable type was developed by Johannes Gutenberg.
Printed books have spread more rapidly than hand-copied manuscripts ever could.
Latin is the common language of scholarship, diplomacy, and the Church in Europe.
Greek texts have been translated into Latin by humanist scholars.
Humanist scholars study classical Greek and Roman texts to recover ancient knowledge.
Universities in Bologna, Paris, and Oxford are among the oldest in Europe.
The University of Bologna is renowned for the study of law.
Roman law as codified under Emperor Justinian remains influential in legal scholarship.
A well-ordered state requires clear laws and consistent enforcement.
Diplomacy conducted through skilled negotiators can prevent costly wars.
Treaties between states are binding only so long as both parties find advantage in them.
A besieged city that cannot be resupplied will eventually fall.
Control of river crossings and mountain passes is strategically vital in warfare.
A prince must balance generosity with the need to maintain his treasury.
Patronage of artists and scholars enhances a ruler's reputation and prestige.
The construction of grand buildings and churches displays a ruler's power and piety.
Brunelleschi designed the dome of the cathedral in Florence.
The cathedral of Florence has the largest masonry dome in the world.
Ancient Roman roads connected the cities of the empire across vast distances.
Roman aqueducts carried water into cities from distant sources.
The Colosseum in Rome was used for public spectacles and gladiatorial combat.
The Roman Senate was the principal governing body of the Roman Republic.
Hannibal of Carthage crossed the Alps with elephants to invade Italy.
The Punic Wars were fought between Rome and Carthage.
Rome destroyed Carthage at the end of the Third Punic War.
The Mongol Empire under Genghis Khan conquered territory from China to Eastern Europe.
Marco Polo traveled from Venice to the court of Kublai Khan in China.
China produces silk, porcelain, and tea.
India produces pepper, cinnamon, and other valuable spices.
The compass aids navigation by indicating magnetic north.
Astrolabes are used to determine latitude by measuring the positions of stars.
Maps and sea charts are essential tools for maritime navigation.
The Mediterranean Sea connects Europe, Africa, and Asia through trade.
The Alps form a natural barrier between Italy and the lands to the north.
The Po River flows across the broad plain of northern Italy.
Sicily is the largest island in the Mediterranean Sea.
Sardinia and Corsica are large islands in the western Mediterranean.
The Rhine and the Danube are major rivers of northern and central Europe.

era_false.statement: The Roman Empire was founded by Alexander the Great in the city of Athens.
The Bible was originally written in the German language.
Constantinople fell to the Mongols in 1453.
The Pope has always resided in the city of London.
Julius Caesar was Emperor of Greece for thirty years.
Water flows upward naturally without any force applied to it.
The sun rises in the west and sets in the east each day.
Charlemagne was a Muslim caliph who ruled from Baghdad.
Gold is lighter than feathers of equal volume.
The Mediterranean Sea is a freshwater lake surrounded by desert.
Aristotle was born in China and wrote exclusively in Arabic.
Fire is cold to the touch and freezes water on contact.
The Nile River flows through the center of France.
Horses have six legs and cannot carry human riders.
The city of Rome was built on a single flat plain with no hills.
Iron is softer than bread and cannot hold a sharp edge.
The Black Death of the fourteenth century killed no one in Europe.
Wine is produced by fermenting stones in barrels of sand.
The Crusades were military campaigns launched by Japan against India.
Snow is warm and causes crops to grow faster in winter.
Virgil wrote the Aeneid in the Spanish language during the twelfth century.
The moon produces its own light brighter than the sun.
Venice is a landlocked city built in the mountains far from any sea.
Wheat grows best when planted in pure salt with no soil.
The Holy Roman Empire was located in southern Africa.
Human blood is bright green and turns blue when exposed to air.
Plato was a Roman general who conquered Persia in battle.
Lead is the most precious metal and rarer than gold or silver.
The Bible contains no mention of Moses or Abraham.
Fish live on dry land and die immediately when placed in water.
The Ottoman Turks were a Christian monastic order from Ireland.
Silk is harvested from the bark of oak trees in England.
The heart is located in the right foot of the human body.
Paris was founded by the ancient Egyptians as a pyramid complex.
Copper and tin combined produce soft clay rather than bronze.
The Gospel of Matthew was written by the philosopher Seneca.
Olives grow exclusively in frozen northern climates near the Arctic.
The Fourth Crusade resulted in the sacking of Rome by Vikings.
Birds have no wings and travel only by swimming through rivers.
The Latin language descended directly from ancient Chinese script.
Dante Alighieri wrote the Divine Comedy in the Greek language.
The Alps are a flat desert region with no mountains whatsoever.
Sheep produce silk and silkworms produce wool in equal measure.
The Hundred Years War was fought between Egypt and China.
The human body has only one bone located in the skull.
Bread is made by baking a mixture of iron filings and vinegar.
Saint Augustine was a Norse pagan priest who rejected Christianity entirely.
The Tiber River runs through the center of Jerusalem.
Grapes ferment naturally into olive oil without human intervention.
The Norman Conquest of England occurred in the year 400 BC.
The earth has three moons visible every clear night.
Thomas Aquinas rejected all of Aristotle's philosophy as worthless nonsense.
Marble is a soft liquid that cannot be carved or shaped.
The Byzantine Empire spoke exclusively Latin and never used Greek.
Horses were first domesticated in the fifteenth century in Scotland.
The printing press was invented in ancient Rome by Cicero himself.
The human stomach is located inside the head behind the eyes.
Silver melts at the same temperature as ice on a winter morning.
The Magna Carta was signed by the Emperor of China in 1215.
Elephants are smaller than mice and native to northern England.
The Danube River flows through the Sahara Desert into the Atlantic.
Oak trees produce fruit identical to oranges in taste and appearance.
The Council of Nicaea was convened by the King of Scotland.
Copper is a transparent material commonly used for cathedral windows.
The Visigoths sacked Rome in the year 1350 during the Renaissance.
Astronomy was invented in the fourteenth century and unknown to the ancients.
The human lungs are solid bones that support the weight of the arms.
Genghis Khan was a Franciscan friar who founded a monastery in Paris.
The Atlantic Ocean is a narrow stream that can be crossed on foot.
Cows lay eggs and chickens produce milk for human consumption.
The fall of the Western Roman Empire occurred in the year 1100.
Glass is produced by melting wool and pouring it into molds.
Saint Peter was a Hindu priest who never visited Rome.
The Pyrenees Mountains separate England from Scotland along their border.
Diamonds are the softest known substance and crumble at a gentle touch.
The Republic of Venice was governed by a hereditary Egyptian pharaoh.
Barley and hops produce bread when brewed together in large vats.
The ancient Greeks had no knowledge of geometry or mathematics.
The Rhine River is located on the continent of Africa near Ethiopia.
Parchment is made from melted iron poured over wooden frames to dry.
The Trojan War was fought between Spain and the Ottoman Empire.
The liver produces no bile and serves no purpose in digestion.
Florence was a major seaport on the coast of the Baltic Sea.
The Julian calendar was created by Attila the Hun in the sixth century.
Camels are aquatic creatures that live exclusively in deep ocean waters.
The Papal States were governed by the King of England throughout history.
Steel is weaker than dry clay and useless for making weapons or tools.
The ancient Olympic Games were held every year in the city of Cairo.
Hippocrates was a medieval French king with no knowledge of medicine.
The earth is shaped like a perfect cube with four flat surfaces.
Vinegar is sweeter than honey and used primarily as a dessert ingredient.
The Colosseum in Rome was built in the fifteenth century by the Medici family.
Lungs serve no role in breathing and exist only to filter blood.
The Hanseatic League was a military alliance of North African kingdoms.
Ptolemy argued that the earth is shaped like a triangle in his writings.
The Dominican Order was founded by a Viking chieftain in Norway.
Granite is softer than butter and melts in warm summer weather.
The Strait of Gibraltar connects the Black Sea to the Indian Ocean.
Galen taught that the human body contains no blood whatsoever.
The University of Bologna was founded in the nineteenth century.
Charlemagne was crowned Emperor by the Sultan of Egypt in Rome.
Salt preserves nothing and causes fresh meat to spoil immediately.
The Parthenon in Athens was built by the ancient Romans in the third century.
The feudal system originated in ancient Egypt under the pharaohs.
Bees produce vinegar inside their hives rather than honey.
The Iberian Peninsula is located north of Scandinavia in the Arctic region.
Euclid's Elements contains no theorems about triangles or circles.
The Black Sea is a mountain range located in central Germany.
The Franciscan Order was founded by King Henry of England as a military force.
Tin is heavier than gold and sinks through solid granite naturally.
The city of Jerusalem is located on an island in the North Sea.
The ancient Romans never built roads or aqueducts of any kind.
Wool comes from fish scales harvested in Mediterranean coastal waters.
The Investiture Controversy was a dispute between China and Japan over trade.
Mercury is safe to drink daily and cures all known diseases instantly.
The Hagia Sophia was built in London by Anglo-Saxon monks in 900.
Pliny the Elder wrote exclusively about mathematics and never discussed nature.
The Danube River is the shortest river in Europe at only one mile long.
The Council of Trent took place in ancient Rome before the birth of Christ.
Chickens are larger than oxen and used to pull heavy plows in fields.

era_believed.statement: The sun revolves around the earth in a perfect circular orbit.
The stars are fixed upon a crystalline sphere surrounding the earth.
The earth sits motionless at the center of the universe.
All celestial bodies are made of a perfect, incorruptible fifth element called aether.
The planets move embedded in solid transparent spheres nested around the earth.
Heavier objects fall faster than lighter objects of the same shape.
There are exactly seven planets including the sun and moon.
Comets are atmospheric disturbances occurring below the sphere of the moon.
The four elements are earth, water, air, and fire.
All matter is composed of varying combinations of the four elements.
Disease is caused by an imbalance of the four bodily humors.
Plague spreads through corrupted air known as miasma.
Bloodletting restores the balance of humors and cures most illnesses.
The heart generates innate heat that warms the entire body.
Blood is continuously produced by the liver from ingested food.
The brain's primary function is to cool the blood.
Veins and arteries carry two fundamentally different types of blood.
The body contains three distinct spirits: natural, vital, and animal.
Wounds can be healed by applying ointment to the weapon that caused them.
Swamps and marshes generate disease through foul vapors alone.
Maggots spontaneously generate from rotting meat.
Mice spontaneously generate from stored grain left in darkness.
Frogs emerge spontaneously from river mud in springtime.
Barnacle geese hatch from driftwood barnacles rather than from eggs.
The salamander can survive and live within fire.
Unicorns exist in distant lands and their horns neutralize poison.
The pelican feeds its young with blood from its own breast.
Basilisks can kill a man with their gaze alone.
Gold is the most perfect metal toward which all base metals naturally strive.
Lead can be transmuted into gold through the proper alchemical process.
The philosopher's stone exists and can perfect any substance it touches.
Gemstones possess inherent medicinal and protective virtues.
Powdered emerald taken internally cures dysentery.
Diamonds can be shattered only by goat's blood.
The position of planets at birth determines a person's temperament and fate.
Eclipses are reliable omens foretelling the deaths of princes.
Comets presage war, famine, and the fall of kingdoms.
The conjunction of Jupiter and Saturn governs the rise and fall of empires.
A competent astrologer can determine the optimal moment to begin a military campaign.
The influence of Mars makes men born under it warlike and choleric.
The earth is roughly six thousand years old based on scriptural genealogy.
All human languages descend from a single original tongue spoken before Babel.
Hebrew is the original language from which all others derived.
The world contains exactly three continents: Europe, Asia, and Africa.
No large landmass exists between western Europe and eastern Asia across the Atlantic.
The Indian Ocean is an enclosed sea bounded by land on its southern edge.
The torrid zone near the equator is too hot for human habitation.
The southern hemisphere is mostly covered by a vast unknown continent balancing the north.
Crocodiles inhabit the river Nile because the warm climate generates them from mud.
Ethiopia is the location of the kingdom of Prester John, a powerful Christian ruler.
The source of the Nile River lies in the Mountains of the Moon.
Cinnamon is harvested from the nests of giant birds in Arabia.
The people of the distant East include races of one-legged men and dog-headed men.
The kingdom of Cathay described by Marco Polo is separate from China.
A northwest sea passage connects the Atlantic to the Pacific through northern America.
The ancient Romans achieved the highest possible form of political organization.
The Roman Republic fell primarily because of moral corruption among its citizens.
A virtuous prince who masters classical wisdom will inevitably govern well.
The ideal state mirrors the political models described by Aristotle and Cicero.
History moves in predictable cycles of growth, corruption, and renewal.
The political wisdom of antiquity surpasses anything discovered by modern thinkers.
A well-constructed republic modeled on Rome can endure indefinitely without structural change.
The mixed constitution combining monarchy, aristocracy, and democracy is inherently the most stable.
Classical Latin rhetoric is the supreme instrument of political persuasion in any culture.
The study of Roman history provides sufficient models for all modern diplomatic situations.
Fortuna governs roughly half of political outcomes regardless of human planning.
A prince's personal virtue is the decisive factor in a state's military success.
Mercenary armies are always inferior to citizen militias in every circumstance.
Swiss pikemen represent the pinnacle of infantry tactics that cannot be surpassed.
Cavalry charges remain the decisive element in pitched battles.
Fortifications built in the medieval style with high thin walls remain effective against cannon.
Greek fire was an unrecoverable lost technology that no modern weapon can replicate.
Crossbows are more militarily effective than firearms in all battlefield conditions.
Gunpowder weapons are too unreliable to ever replace traditional siege engines entirely.
Naval warfare will always depend primarily on ramming and boarding tactics.
Armored knights on horseback remain the dominant force in European warfare.
A city under siege will inevitably fall once its food supply is exhausted, with no other outcome.
The Ottoman Empire's military strength derives primarily from the personal valor of the Sultan.
The proper formation for infantry is the deep phalanx described by ancient Greek writers.
War elephants remain a viable and fearsome weapon in eastern armies.
Venice's political stability comes from its constitution being a perfect mixed government.
The Pope holds legitimate temporal authority over all Christian monarchs.
The Holy Roman Emperor holds rightful supreme secular authority over all Christendom.
The balance of power among Italian states can be maintained indefinitely through skilled diplomacy.
Dynastic legitimacy based on bloodline is the only stable basis for sovereign authority.
A state's wealth is measured entirely by its holdings of gold and silver.
Trade between nations is a zero-sum exchange where one side always loses.
Lending money at interest is inherently economically destructive to a commonwealth.
Silk can only be produced in China and the distant East.
Spices are extremely rare because they grow only in a few remote tropical locations.
The wealth of the Indies is essentially inexhaustible.
A prince who controls the loyalty of the nobility controls the entire population.
Republics are inherently unsuited to governing large territories.
Diplomatic treaties between Christian and Muslim rulers can never be legitimate.
Women are biologically incapable of the rational judgment required for statecraft.
A ruler's physiognomy reliably reveals his character and fitness to govern.
The climate of northern Europe makes its people inherently less intelligent than southern Europeans.
People living in cold climates are naturally more courageous but less cunning than those in warm climates.
African peoples south of the Sahara have produced no complex civilizations or written traditions.
The Chinese empire is a static civilization that has never undergone significant political change.
The Japanese islands are fabulously rich in gold as reported by Marco Polo.
The Great Khan still rules a unified Mongol empire across Asia.
The Ptolemaic measurement of the earth's circumference is the most accurate available.
India is reachable by sailing a short distance west from the Canary Islands.
The Atlantic Ocean is relatively narrow between Europe and Asia.
Tides are caused by the breathing of the earth or the influence of the moon's light.
Earthquakes are caused by winds trapped in underground caverns.
Thunder is produced by dry exhalations igniting in the upper atmosphere.
Fossils of sea creatures found on mountains were deposited by the biblical flood.
The Milky Way is a phenomenon occurring in the earth's upper atmosphere.
Amber attracts straw because of an occult sympathetic quality inherent in the substance.
Magnets attract iron because of a hidden sympathy between the two substances.
The human body contains exactly the same proportions as a perfectly designed building.
The veins of the body correspond directly to rivers on the earth's surface.
Whales are the largest creatures that have ever existed or that are physically possible.
The mandrake root screams when pulled from the ground and can kill the person who uproots it.
Consuming ground horn of rhinoceros is an effective antidote to poison.
Bezoar stones found in animal stomachs are universal antidotes to all poisons.
A lodestone loses its magnetic power if rubbed with garlic.
The torpedo fish numbs a fisherman's hand through an occult quality transmitted along the line.

modern_true.statement: The Treaty of Westphalia in 1648 established the principle of state sovereignty in European international law.
The Congress of Vienna in 1815 redrew the political map of Europe after Napoleon's defeat.
The balance of power doctrine was formally theorized by European diplomats during the eighteenth century.
Cardinal Richelieu centralized French royal authority by systematically dismantling Huguenot political power after 1624.
The Peace of Augsburg in 1555 allowed German princes to choose between Lutheranism and Catholicism for their territories.
Niccolò Machiavelli's works were placed on the Papal Index of Forbidden Books in 1559.
The Dutch Republic gained formal independence from Spain through the Treaty of Münster in 1648.
Hugo Grotius published foundational texts on international law in the early seventeenth century.
The concept of raison d'état was developed as explicit political doctrine in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries.
Jean Bodin formulated the modern theory of absolute sovereignty in his 1576 work Six Books of the Commonwealth.
Thomas Hobbes published Leviathan in 1651, arguing for a social contract under an absolute sovereign.
The Edict of Nantes in 1598 granted French Protestants substantial civil rights and was revoked in 1685.
Diplomatic immunity became codified in European practice during the seventeenth century.
Permanent resident ambassadors became standard in European diplomacy only after the mid-sixteenth century.
The Ottoman siege of Vienna in 1683 marked the furthest Ottoman military advance into central Europe.
The Spanish Armada was defeated by England in 1588.
The Thirty Years' War killed an estimated one-third of the German-speaking population of central Europe.
Cardinal Mazarin continued Richelieu's centralizing policies during the minority of Louis XIV.
The War of the Spanish Succession ended with the Treaty of Utrecht in 1713.
Frederick the Great of Prussia wrote Anti-Machiavel in 1740 criticizing Machiavelli's political philosophy.
The partitions of Poland in 1772, 1793, and 1795 erased Poland from the map of Europe entirely.
Montesquieu proposed the separation of governmental powers into legislative, executive, and judicial branches in 1748.
The concept of the professional standing army replaced feudal levies across Europe during the seventeenth century.
The French Revolution of 1789 abolished the feudal privileges of the French aristocracy.
Napoleon Bonaparte crowned himself Emperor of France in 1804.
The Holy Roman Empire was formally dissolved in 1806.
The Concert of Europe system maintained relative great-power peace from 1815 to 1853.
Clausewitz published On War posthumously in 1832, defining war as the continuation of politics by other means.
The unification of Italy was completed in 1871 with Rome as its capital.
The unification of Germany under Prussian leadership was proclaimed at Versailles in 1871.
Otto von Bismarck used a system of alliances to maintain European balance of power in the late nineteenth century.
The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in 1914 triggered the outbreak of World War One.
The League of Nations was established in 1920 as the first global intergovernmental organization for maintaining peace.
The United Nations was founded in 1945 after the failure of the League of Nations.
The Geneva Conventions established modern rules governing the treatment of prisoners of war and civilians.
Nuclear deterrence doctrine shaped Cold War diplomacy between the United States and the Soviet Union after 1945.
The North Atlantic Treaty Organization was founded in 1949 as a collective defense alliance.
The European Economic Community was established by the Treaty of Rome in 1957.
Patronage systems in European courts expanded dramatically under Louis XIV at Versailles after 1682.
The Medici family lost and regained control of Florence multiple times during the sixteenth century.
Pope Paul III convened the Council of Trent in 1545 to address Protestant criticisms of the Catholic Church.
The Jesuit order was founded by Ignatius of Loyola and officially approved by the Pope in 1540.
The Index Librorum Prohibitorum was established in 1559 to censor printed works deemed heretical.
Philip II of Spain built the Escorial palace as a combined monastery, royal residence, and administrative center.
The Star Chamber court in England was abolished by Parliament in 1641.
The English Civil War resulted in the execution of King Charles I in 1649.
The Glorious Revolution of 1688 established parliamentary supremacy over the English monarchy.
John Locke published Two Treatises of Government in 1689, arguing that legitimate rule requires the consent of the governed.
The Bill of Rights of 1689 permanently limited the powers of the English crown.
Voltaire's writings in the eighteenth century attacked religious intolerance and advocated civil liberties across Europe.
The Rosetta Stone was discovered by French soldiers in Egypt in 1799.
Jean-François Champollion deciphered Egyptian hieroglyphics in 1822 using the Rosetta Stone.
Heinrich Schliemann excavated the site believed to be ancient Troy in the 1870s.
The Library of Ashurbanipal containing thousands of cuneiform tablets was excavated at Nineveh in the nineteenth century.
Pompeii was rediscovered and excavation began in 1748, revealing a preserved Roman city.
The Dead Sea Scrolls were discovered in caves near the Dead Sea beginning in 1947.
Linear B script was deciphered by Michael Ventris in 1952, revealing it as an early form of Greek.
Copernicus published his heliocentric model of the solar system in 1543.
Galileo observed the moons of Jupiter through a telescope in 1610.
Johannes Kepler published his laws of planetary motion between 1609 and 1619.
Isaac Newton published the Principia Mathematica in 1687, describing the laws of motion and universal gravitation.
William Harvey described the circulation of blood through the human body in 1628.
The microscope was developed in the late sixteenth century and refined by Antonie van Leeuwenhoek in the seventeenth century.
Robert Hooke first used the word "cell" to describe biological structures in 1665.
Antoine Lavoisier identified oxygen and established modern chemistry in the late eighteenth century.
Edward Jenner developed the first successful smallpox vaccine in 1796.
Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1859, proposing evolution by natural selection.
Louis Pasteur demonstrated that microorganisms cause fermentation and disease in the 1860s.
Gregor Mendel published his laws of genetic inheritance based on pea plant experiments in 1866.
The structure of DNA was described by Watson and Crick in 1953.
Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928, leading to the development of antibiotics.
The first successful human heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in 1967.
The Human Genome Project completed its mapping of the human genome in 2003.
The Americas were colonized by multiple European powers throughout the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries.
The transatlantic slave trade forcibly transported millions of Africans to the Americas between the sixteenth and nineteenth centuries.
The Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire was completed by Hernán Cortés in 1521.
The Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire was completed by Francisco Pizarro in the 1530s.
Ferdinand Magellan's expedition completed the first circumnavigation of the Earth in 1522.
Australia was mapped by Dutch explorers in the seventeenth century and colonized by Britain beginning in 1788.
The Suez Canal opened in 1869, connecting the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea.
The Panama Canal opened in 1914, connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans.
The American Declaration of Independence was signed in 1776.
The Haitian Revolution of 1791 to 1804 produced the first successful slave revolt leading to an independent nation.
Simón Bolívar led independence movements across South America in the early nineteenth century.
The Berlin Conference of 1884 formalized European colonial partition of Africa.
India gained independence from British rule in 1947.
The Berlin Wall fell in 1989, symbolizing the end of the Cold War division of Europe.
The Soviet Union dissolved in 1991, ending the Cold War.
Johannes Gutenberg's printing press spread rapidly across Europe after 1500, transforming the dissemination of knowledge.
The first successful steam engine for industrial use was developed by Thomas Newcomen in 1712.
James Watt significantly improved the steam engine in the 1760s, accelerating the Industrial Revolution.
The first commercial railway opened between Stockton and Darlington in England in 1825.
The telegraph was developed by Samuel Morse in the 1830s, enabling near-instantaneous long-distance communication.
The telephone was patented by Alexander Graham Bell in 1876.
The Wright brothers achieved the first powered heavier-than-air flight in 1903.
The first artificial satellite, Sputnik, was launched by the Soviet Union in 1957.
Humans first landed on the Moon during the Apollo 11 mission in 1969.
The internet became publicly accessible in the 1990s, transforming global communication.
The first programmable electronic computer was built during the 1940s.
Electricity was harnessed for widespread public use in the late nineteenth century.
The theory of general relativity was published by Albert Einstein in 1915.
Quantum mechanics was developed in the early twentieth century by Planck, Bohr, Heisenberg, and Schrödinger.
The periodic table of elements was organized by Dmitri Mendeleev in 1869.
Radioactivity was discovered by Henri Becquerel in 1896.
The atomic bomb was first detonated in a test at Alamogordo, New Mexico, in July 1945.
Nuclear power plants began generating electricity for civilian use in the 1950s.
The abolition of slavery in the British Empire was enacted by Parliament in 1833.
The Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution abolished slavery in 1865.
Women gained the right to vote in New Zealand in 1893, the first country to grant universal female suffrage.
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted by the United Nations General Assembly in 1948.
Antarctica was first sighted by European explorers in 1820.
The source of the Nile River was identified by European explorers in the nineteenth century.
Plate tectonics theory was widely accepted by geologists in the 1960s.
The ozone hole over Antarctica was first detected by scientists in 1985.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into orbit in 1990.
The existence of galaxies beyond the Milky Way was confirmed by Edwin Hubble in 1924.
Photography was invented in the early nineteenth century by Niépce and Daguerre.
The first motion pictures were shown publicly by the Lumière brothers in 1895.
The Treaty of Westphalia in 1648 established the principle of state sovereignty as a foundation of international diplomacy.
The Congress of Vienna in 1815 redrew the political map of Europe after the Napoleonic Wars.

modern_false.statement: The Treaty of Tordesillas was signed in 1497 between Spain and Portugal to divide newly discovered lands.
Vasco da Gama reached India by sea in 1502, landing first at the port of Bombay.
Martin Luther nailed his Ninety-Five Theses to the church door in Wittenberg in 1521.
The printing press was introduced to England by Thomas More in 1479.
Nicolaus Copernicus published De revolutionibus orbium coelestium in 1538.
The Spanish Armada was defeated by English naval forces in 1592.
Ferdinand Magellan completed the first circumnavigation of the globe, returning to Spain in 1522.
The Edict of Nantes was issued by Henry IV of France in 1592 to grant rights to Protestants.
William Harvey described the circulation of blood in a treatise published in 1618.
The Peace of Westphalia was signed in 1652, ending the Thirty Years' War.
Galileo Galilei first observed the moons of Jupiter through a telescope in 1614.
The English Bill of Rights was enacted in 1685 following the Glorious Revolution.
Isaac Newton published Principia Mathematica in 1692 through Cambridge University Press.
The Act of Union joining England and Scotland was passed in 1703.
Johannes Kepler formulated his three laws of planetary motion by 1605.
The first permanent English settlement in North America was established at Jamestown in 1604.
Cardinal Richelieu served as chief minister to Louis XIV of France beginning in 1628.
The Ottoman Empire captured Constantinople in 1503 under Sultan Bayezid II.
Francis Bacon published Novum Organum in 1625, outlining his empirical method.
The Dutch East India Company was founded in 1598 as the first joint-stock company.
René Descartes published Meditations on First Philosophy in 1637.
The Great Fire of London occurred in 1669, destroying much of the medieval city.
The Treaty of Utrecht was signed in 1709, ending the War of the Spanish Succession.
Blaise Pascal invented the mechanical calculator in 1639 while working for his father.
The Mayflower landed at Plymouth Rock in 1618 carrying English Separatists.
Tycho Brahe built his famous observatory on the island of Ven in 1580.
The Petition of Right was presented to Charles I of England in 1625.
Andreas Vesalius published De humani corporis fabrica in 1540 in Venice.
The Battle of Lepanto took place in 1574 between Ottoman and Holy League forces.
Hugo Grotius published On the Law of War and Peace in 1620.
The steam engine was first patented by James Watt in 1763.
The Declaration of Independence was signed on July 2, 1776, in Philadelphia.
Antoine Lavoisier discovered oxygen in 1778 and named it the following year.
The Rosetta Stone was discovered by French soldiers in Egypt in 1801.
The first successful smallpox vaccine was administered by Edward Jenner in 1793.
Benjamin Franklin conducted his famous kite experiment in 1756 in Boston.
The Louisiana Purchase was completed in 1806 for eighteen million dollars.
The Congress of Vienna concluded in 1818 after redrawing the map of Europe.
Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1856.
The first transatlantic telegraph cable was successfully laid in 1854.
The Suez Canal was completed and opened to shipping in 1873.
Alexander Graham Bell patented the telephone in 1878 in New York City.
The Emancipation Proclamation was issued by Abraham Lincoln in 1864.
Thomas Edison invented the practical incandescent light bulb in 1882.
The Berlin Conference that partitioned Africa took place in 1888.
Marie Curie discovered radium in 1901 while working at the University of Berlin.
The Wright brothers achieved powered flight at Kitty Hawk in 1901.
The Panama Canal was completed and opened for transit in 1917.
Guglielmo Marconi sent the first transatlantic radio signal in 1899.
Albert Einstein published his special theory of relativity in 1903.
The Treaty of Versailles was signed in 1921, formally ending World War I.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1925 at Oxford University.
The stock market crash that triggered the Great Depression occurred in October 1931.
The first successful organ transplant was a kidney transplant performed in 1958.
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted by the United Nations in 1951.
DNA's double helix structure was described by Watson and Crick in 1951.
The first human-made satellite, Sputnik, was launched by the Soviet Union in 1955.
Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space on April 12, 1963.
The Berlin Wall was constructed in 1959 to divide East and West Berlin.
Neil Armstrong walked on the Moon on July 20, 1971.
The first successful heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in 1965.
The internet's predecessor, ARPANET, sent its first message in 1972.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred in 1984 in the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic.
The Human Genome Project was completed in 2001, two years ahead of schedule.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into orbit in 1988.
The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1993 at MIT.
The fall of the Berlin Wall took place on November 9, 1991.
The Maastricht Treaty establishing the European Union was signed in 1990.
Dolly the sheep, the first cloned mammal, was born in 1994 in Scotland.
The Kyoto Protocol on climate change was adopted in 1999.
The first iPhone was released by Apple in 2005, revolutionizing mobile technology.
The Mars rover Curiosity landed on Mars in 2014.
The Paris Climate Agreement was signed by world leaders in 2017.
Hernán Cortés conquered the Aztec Empire and captured Tenochtitlan in 1525.
Francisco Pizarro defeated the Inca Empire at the Battle of Cajamarca in 1536.
The Council of Trent convened in 1542 and lasted for twenty-two years.
Sir Walter Raleigh established the colony of Roanoke in 1582.
The Gunpowder Plot to blow up the English Parliament was uncovered in 1607.
Rembrandt painted The Night Watch in 1638 for the Amsterdam civic guard.
The English Civil War began in 1645 when Parliament raised an army against Charles I.
Oliver Cromwell was declared Lord Protector of England in 1651.
The Great Plague of London killed an estimated 150,000 people in 1668.
Peter the Great founded the city of St. Petersburg in 1698.
The Seven Years' War began in 1754 and involved every major European power.
The Boston Tea Party took place in December 1775 as a protest against British taxation.
The French Revolution began with the storming of the Bastille on July 14, 1791.
Napoleon Bonaparte crowned himself Emperor of France in 1806 at Notre-Dame Cathedral.
The Battle of Waterloo was fought in 1813, ending Napoleon's rule permanently.
The Monroe Doctrine was proclaimed by President James Monroe in 1825.
The Treaty of Nanking ending the First Opium War was signed in 1845.
The Crimean War began in 1856 between Russia and an alliance led by Britain and France.
The unification of Italy was completed in 1867 under King Victor Emmanuel II.
Otto von Bismarck unified Germany in 1874 following the Franco-Prussian War.
The Meiji Restoration in Japan began in 1871, modernizing the country rapidly.
The Spanish-American War took place in 1901, resulting in Spain losing its colonies.
The Boxer Rebellion in China occurred in 1903 against foreign imperial influence.
The Russian Revolution of 1917 began with the storming of the Winter Palace in March.
The League of Nations was established in 1922 following the end of World War I.
The Balfour Declaration supporting a Jewish homeland in Palestine was issued in 1919.
The Munich Agreement allowing German annexation of the Sudetenland was signed in 1936.
Japan attacked Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1943, bringing the United States into World War II.
The United Nations was officially founded in 1947 in San Francisco.
NATO was established in 1951 as a military alliance against Soviet expansion.
The Korean War ended with an armistice signed in 1955.
The Cuban Missile Crisis brought the world to the brink of nuclear war in 1964.
The Gulf of Tonkin Resolution authorizing military force in Vietnam was passed in 1962.
The Camp David Accords between Egypt and Israel were signed in 1976.
The Falklands War between Britain and Argentina took place in 1984.
The Soviet Union officially dissolved on December 25, 1989.
The Good Friday Agreement bringing peace to Northern Ireland was signed in 1996.
The September 11 attacks on the United States occurred in 2003.
The Arab Spring uprisings began in Tunisia in 2009 and spread across the Middle East.
The United Kingdom voted to leave the European Union in a 2018 referendum.
Michelangelo completed the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel in 1516 under Pope Leo X.
Leonardo da Vinci painted the Mona Lisa between 1507 and 1515 while living in Rome.
William Shakespeare wrote Hamlet in 1605 during the reign of James I.
The first public opera house opened in Naples in 1635.
Johann Sebastian Bach composed the Brandenburg Concertos in 1728.
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart composed his final symphony in 1793 shortly before his death.
Ludwig van Beethoven premiered his Ninth Symphony in 1822 in Berlin.
